sentences.text: In 1944, when I came home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we hunted collaborators, but we didn't find them.
So I was at home for a few months, a couple of months, and I decided to go to the army.
Not that I decided, but there was a law that everybody must join the army.
And I went, and I joined the army.

And while I joined, I requested that, as I was a partisan, I wanted to go to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] officers' school, because I didn't have-- I didn't think anything, so I thought maybe the military would be a good thing for me.
So they have assigned me for three months to officers' school, and I went for three months.
And the officers' school was called a city, Rowne.
It was a big city.
And before the war, there were about 25,000 Jews.
So I went there for three months.
And after three months, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they sent me on the front.
Do you remember when you were there, were there other Jews at the time?
There were a few Jewish fellows, yes.
Yes.
In Rowne?
Yes.
Because while I was in the officers' school, I-- it just came to my mind while you asked me.
On a day that I had free, we didn't have any classes there, I went to look for Jewish people.
So I met some people in Rowne.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the street was called-- in Polish, it was called Trzeciego Maja.
That means the street was called The Third of May.
And on the second floor, there were Jewish people.
Not from Rowne, but from the province, from little towns.
And I used to come there, have coffee with them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
One day, I met a Jewish captain, an army man.
And I say, I have some friends upstairs.
Why don't we go and have a drink?
He says, OK.
So we went upstairs, and we had some vodka there and some salami.
We ate and drank.
And for some reason, I went down from the second floor.
I went down on the street.
And I saw in a corner there is a fellow beating up another fellow.
So I got interested.
So I went up, and as I came closer, the fellow that was beaten up was a Jewish fellow, by a Ukrainian nationalist.
But the Jewish fellow looked at me, and he yelled out, 'amcho?' 'Amcho' was like a parole, 'Are you Jewish?' I say, yes.
He says, he wants to kill me, that Ukraine.
I turned out my [INAUDIBLE], and I cut down the fellow over the legs with-- the fellow that beating up the Jewish fellow-- cut in his-- through his legs.
I went upstairs, and I mentioned to the captain.
The captain says, let's better leave here.
And we left.
I went to my place, to school.
And he went.
So after three months being in school there, they transferred me on the front.
But at that time, Poland was divided-- half to Germany and half to the Russians.
That's where the front started.
Stopped at Warsaw.
And they stopped there till January the 14th, 1945.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1945, January the 14th, there was an order to attack the Germans and push them out from Poland.
And that didn't take too long.
Two weeks the Russian army pushed them away up to the river, Oder.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was in the infantry.
So while they were pushed away, the army moved.
They moved farther on German territory.
I remember coming to Katowice.
A city, Katowice.
On my right side, there was a German tank.
He had burned-- burning.
And a German soldier, a big one, was lying beside the tank.
So I went up to the German soldier, and I stepped with my foot on him to see if he's frozen, or he's still alive, or dead.
He was dead, but not frozen yet.
So it must have happened very short.
Then we went from Katowice.
There is a place called Hindenburg in Gliwice.
And there is some-- some-- a lot of trees there on the road, on both side.
And I remember seeing dead bodies on the side, and they were wearing striped uniforms.
I didn't know that they were even Jews, but they were concentration-- [COUGHS] pardon me-- they were concentration people.
And I was told this after.
Till we came to the Oder, to the River Oder-- [COUGHS]-- and the Germans had fortification there.
And that was end of January.
And the Germans were holding us up to May, till the Russians broke through and went in to cover Berlin.
[COUGHS] Now, I was not in Berlin, but I was on the river, Oder.
And in May was the war finished.
But I know that I have a mother, and I have a sister, so I had to go back and find them.
So when I came to Poland from Germany already, and I came from-- on the Polish territory-- a matter of fact, I came to Krakow.
And I was yet in military uniform, because I wasn't yet discharged from the army.
So I thought, I'll go back to my hometown and get my mother and my sister.
Where my brother was in the army, I didn't know at that time.
But eventually I got in touch with my mother and with my sister, and we-- they came actually to Lwow, Lemberg.
And I got idea that they're waiting there for me to come and take them home.
They should come to Poland.
So as a military man-- [COUGHS] as a military man, I didn't need any visas or passport, as long as I wore the uniform.
And at that time, a lot of soldiers were moving back and forward.
So I came to Lwow, and I met my mother and my sister.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I brought them over to Poland, to a city called Jaroslaw.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Being in Jaroslaw, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the military police caught me, and I did not have a discharge from the army, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so they took me with them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they didn't treat me as a criminal, but in my mind, at that time, I wasn't treated the way I supposed to be.
And in no time at all, [COUGHS] from the military police, I walked out and I went away.
And nobody was after me.
No one.
And I went in-- in a house that there was a volksdeutsche living.
You know what a volksdeutsche is.
And there was a lady in the house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she got shaking.
So I told her, I'm hungry.
And I have a wound on my leg, because my-- I had a blister, so I was limping a little bit.
So she took water and washed my foot and make to eat.
But I was afraid.
I was looking around.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So while I was looking around, under-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what would you call  it?
A [NON-ENGLISH].
I find there a picture of a Gestapo young fellow.
So I asked her who this is, and she says, that's my son.
But she says, sons used to kill their parents if they didn't obey the law of the Nazis.
[COUGHS] Anyways, I took off my military suit, and I told-- told her to give me a civilian suit.
And she gave me her son's suit.
And I left her the military suit, and I put on a civil suit.
And I went to Krakow.
And while I was in Krakow, I got a hold of a Polish soldier, because my mother was in Jaroslaw with my sister.
And I say, tell my mother and my sister that I'm in Jaroslaw-- in Krakow.
Let them come.
And sure enough, they came to Krakow.
And we settled for a while in Krakow.
There was a lot of Jewish people already from around the province, from-- from all over.
But that wasn't home for us.
That wasn't life for us.
We didn't work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we decided to leave Poland.
When was that?
That was already--
To leave Poland.
That was in 1945.
It must have been in August.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we-- where do you go?
In May.
May the 7th, was it, I think.
I was in Krakow at that time.
[COUGHS]
So there was no place for us in Poland, so we decided to go.
But where do you go?
So at that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a organization called the Bricha.
Have you heard?
There were groups of people that they tried to bring over people-- from Russia, from Poland, from Czechoslovakia, from everywhere-- to Israel.
But first of all, to bring them out from Poland.
And there were many, many people-- Jewish people-- that the Bricha came and organized us, and said, look, we're going to Czechoslovakia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And right after the war, people were moving from one border to the other border.
You didn't get visas or passports.
So we were organized, maybe 200 people, and we went to Czechoslovakia with the Bricha.
From there, Czechoslovakia was not home, so we went to Austria.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came in to Austria in a city called Villach.
We walked.
We walked.
Took days, but that's the way.
We walked.
For an example, from Poland to cross the Czechoslovakia border was maybe 100, 150 kilometers, because it was very close.
So we walked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we-- we came to that Austrian city, Villach.
That was right on the border.
On the right was Yugoslavia, on the left was Italy.
A lot of people from our group crossed the mountains to Yugoslavia.
A lot of people went right down to the-- to the train and got to Italy.
So I remember our group [COUGHS] went down, and there were box cars of trains.
And the fellows that they lead us said, crawl into the boxcar and close the door and stay there.
So we crawled in.
We didn't know where they'll go-- east or west or south.
We didn't.
Or they're going to stay there.
My mother, my sister, my brother, already all together.
He joined us in Krakow.
He got to know that we're there, and he came to Krakow.
And we traveled south all together.
So we got into the trains, in the boxcars, and we were sitting there.
And we had dry bread.
We had water.
It was organized well.
We were sitting there maybe 20 hours in this boxcar till they started to move.
Next, we find out that we're in-- what was it called?
That we're in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the boxcars opened up, and we all went out.
And the fellows from the Bricha say there are DP camps.
We'll put you in the DP camps.
And further we will notify you in the DP camps.
So you have a room and you have food.
Because at that time, when they put you-- [COUGHS] put you in the DP camps, used to be called the UNRRA.
I don't know if you remember the UNRRA.
And they used to ship, in the joint from the United States to the DP camps, food and clothes.
Helping out.
And when the time will come, we'll take you to Israel.
At that time, it was Palestine, because that was still in 1945.
So we came to a DP camp, and we settled in the DP camp.
Trani.
Matter of fact, I have a picture and the number of that thing.
And we were sitting there two years in that DP camp, Trani.
But we did not like it there very much, because there were-- in the morning, you get up, you go to the kitchen, you get coffee, you go up, and you get lunch.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So as my brother was a pre-war photographer, and I had already an idea a little bit photography, so we got a camera.
And we started to take pictures in the camp.
And sure enough, it was very successful, because anybody in camp find a relative in Israel or United States or in Brazil, everywhere, so they wanted pictures to mail it to them.
So we moved out from the camp privately.
We rented a house from an Italian.
But we were registered in camp, because to emigrate you have to be registered in a camp.
And in the day time, we used to drive up to the camps and take pictures.
Next day, develop them, deliver them, and cash in-- cash in on it.
But living in Italy, you can never become a citizen.
You're all the time so-called straniero.
You're a-- what do you-- there's a word in English.
That's right.
You cannot get a job legally, because there wasn't enough work for the Italians.
But as we used to work for ourself, take pictures, so we made some money.
We were able to afford to live in a Italian home, pay them the rent.
And at the same time, we used to develop the pictures there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So right away, after two years, people have changed their minds.
They don't want to go to Israel.
If you have an uncle in the United States, or you have a auntie in Canada, or you have a brother or sister, right away people started to get affidavits to go to the States, to go to Canada.
But I didn't have anywhere-- anyone in the world.
So a fellow from my hometown [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went to Canada, to Winnipeg, matter of fact.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I told him-- I asked him, maybe you'll find some people from our hometown.
Maybe they will willing to make affidavit for us to come to Canada.
Sure enough, he left, contact some people in Winnipeg, and right away we get a letter from Canada to-- to Italy that they remember my father when he was alive, and they remember my mother.
They're going to make a permit for us to come to Canada.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that was Yehudi Antilov's father.
He had a farm in St.
Vital.
So my [INAUDIBLE] from Winnipeg went to him and they told him the story.
And he made an affidavit to-- for us to come to work on his farm.
By the way, when I was in Italy, they said that a farmer can emigrate very easily to Italy.
So I went to school, to agricultural school, in Italy for over a year to get a certificate that-- I still have it-- to get a certificate that I'm a-- that I know how to farm.
And sure enough, we came to Winnipeg.
First-- it first took two years.
We were in the north, called Trani [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And around Trani, that place, there was the Adriatic Sea.
And on the Adriatic Sea, there were the Fascists' summer homes.
But when the Fascists were destroyed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so the DP camps were in their place there.
But again, you live in one room, because in that Trani camp, at that time, it was like a transit camp.
There were better than 2,500 people coming and going.
There were younger people that they were brought.
They were there a week, a month, two months, on a boat into Israel.
Well, I did not have any problems.
After one year, I spoke Italian perfect.
And I used to dress like they do.
And I used to go out with them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they didn't know even what a Jew is.
I remember when I landed in Italy, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I went up to Italians to tell them who I am.
And I say, I'm a Juda.
They didn't know what a Juda means.
I said, I'm Yid.
They didn't know, till months later they did find out that we have-- that we are Ebraici.
And that was the word for a Jew, Ebraico.
But they were OK.
Never had any problems there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Just to go out with Italian girls, with Italian boys.
My brother left Italy to Canada from Genoa.
I didn't want to go to Genoa.
I wanted to go to France, to Paris, because I thought, I'm going to Canada, so far.
I'll never have a chance to see Paris.
So while I'm there, may as well see Paris.
So in Rome, I went into the French consulate.
And I tell them I'm going to Canada-- and I showed them my passport and my visa-- but I would like to visit France before I get.
So, no problem.
For the visa, I paid him-- I can't remember how much-- how much it is.
And I took the electric train, and I drove in-- we drove into-- to Paris.
And I spent a month in Paris.
There was the Joint, the Jewish organization.

They help people that they didn't have any money or-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so we used to go there in the day times, sit, and read brochures from Israel, the newspaper, because they used to get Hebrew papers and Jewish paper.
And I spent a month.
And I thought I had enough, so I went, in Paris, and I bought two tickets, for myself and my mother, and we went to the  Port Le Havre, and we took the boat.
And we came to Canada.
My sister remained in Italy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She had a boyfriend, and she didn't want to leave him.
But apparently the boyfriend couldn't emigrate to Canada, so she stayed another six or eight months in Canada.
And while us being in-- she stayed in Italy.
And while us being in Canada, we send her a permit, and she came to Canada.
Now, I was lucky.
When I came to Canada, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't have any money.
I didn't have any job.
But apparently, I was approached on the third day to be in Canada if I would like to work in a camera store.
Winnipeg.
And I said, sure.
I would like to work, but I don't know the language.
You'll learn.
So in Winnipeg used to be a camera store called Strains on Smith Street.
And they hired me right away, the first day, to come to work.
How old were you at the time?
When I came to Canada? '49, I was already 28 years old.
OK.
